slides.title: TelePreview : A User-Friendly Teleoperation System with Virtual Arm Assistance for Enhanced Effectiveness Website Jingxiang Guo * , Jiayu Luo * , Zhenyu Wei * , Yiwen Hou , Zhixuan Xu , Xiaoyi Lin, Chongkai Gao , Lin Sha
slides.selectionReason: TelePreview title and motivation

slides.title: Why we make TelePreview? Teleoperation systems lack intuitive feedback to help operators understand how their commands will translate into robot actions . ❓ “ It already fails when I notice that… ”
slides.selectionReason: TelePreview title and motivation

slides.title: Why we make TelePreview? ❓ “ It already fails when I notice that… ” Reset The Environment Again Get the success demo Time Consuming!
slides.selectionReason: TelePreview motivation and virtual-arm concept

slides.title: What we do in TelePreview? Use a “ virtual arm ” as a preview of the results produced by our teleoperation; by toggling an I/O switch, decide whether to send the previewed joint-angle states to the real robotic arm, allo
slides.selectionReason: TelePreview motivation and virtual-arm concept

slides.title: TelePreview Framework Our entire teleoperation system supports a wide range of input devices for capturing hand poses and wrist positions. These inputs are unified through SMPL-X, then undergo joint-to-joint mapping with
slides.selectionReason: TelePreview detailed framework, experiments, and conclusion

slides.title: TelePreview Framework We ultimately chose an IMU motion-capture setup to obtain wrist information via forward kinematics, paired with a data glove to capture each finger joint’s angles. This setup costs around $1,000, ma
slides.selectionReason: TelePreview detailed framework, experiments, and conclusion

slides.title: TelePreview Workflow We acquire hand and wrist data from our input devices: the wrist is driven by front–back residual control, and the hand pose is first mapped joint-to-joint before passing through a self-collision-avo
slides.selectionReason: TelePreview detailed framework, experiments, and conclusion

slides.title: TelePreview —— Teleoperation Pipeline What is the purpose of the “Joint2Joint Map” here? The degrees of freedom we capture with the glove exceed those of the dexterous hand, so we only select the human joints that corres
slides.selectionReason: TelePreview detailed framework, experiments, and conclusion

slides.title: TelePreview —— Teleoperation Pipeline Why don’t we just track fingertip positions and then solve inverse kinematics? Because the robotic hand and the human hand differ in size, relying solely on each fingertip’s 6D pose
slides.selectionReason: TelePreview detailed framework, experiments, and conclusion

slides.title: TelePreview —— Teleoperation Pipeline When we tried using open-source vision-based teleoperation methods, we encountered self-collisions on the LeapHand dexterous hand, which hindered real-world deployment.
slides.selectionReason: TelePreview detailed framework, experiments, and conclusion

slides.title: TelePreview —— Teleoperation Pipeline So we trained Non-collision retargeting network. We feed the current dexterous‐hand configuration (joint‐angle vector qpos ) into our system. If it would cause a self‐collision, we w
slides.selectionReason: TelePreview detailed framework, experiments, and conclusion

slides.title: TelePreview —— Teleoperation Pipeline For different dexterous hands, this network only requires configuration sampling and a one-time training, so there’s no re-optimization overhead during teleoperation. As a result, ou
slides.selectionReason: TelePreview detailed framework, experiments, and conclusion

slides.title: TelePreview —— Preview Pipeline Our virtual image is aligned with the real robotic arm using the AprilTag visible in the camera’s view. Step 1: Hand–Eye Calibration – Compute transform between robot base frame and fixed
slides.selectionReason: TelePreview detailed framework, experiments, and conclusion

slides.title: TelePreview —— Preview Pipeline We use PyRender to render the “virtual image” according to the previously defined configuration, then directly overlay it onto our real robotic arm. We calibrate the virtual camera’s intri
slides.selectionReason: TelePreview detailed framework, experiments, and conclusion

slides.title: TelePreview —— Experiment Pick & Place Hang Cup Stack Pour Box Rotation
slides.selectionReason: TelePreview detailed framework, experiments, and conclusion

slides.title: TelePreview —— Experiment
slides.selectionReason: TelePreview detailed framework, experiments, and conclusion

slides.title: TelePreview achieves higher success rates than all baselines. While Telekinesis, AnyTeleop, and Open Teach handle simpler, short-horizon tasks, they often struggle with complex manipulations requiring subtle wrist rotati
slides.selectionReason: TelePreview detailed framework, experiments, and conclusion

slides.title: TelePreview —— Experiment We validated TelePreview on a UFactory xArm fitted with three end-effectors— a 16-DoF LeapHand, a 1-DoF parallel-jaw gripper, and a binary vacuum gripper—requiring only minor parameter adjusted
slides.selectionReason: TelePreview detailed framework, experiments, and conclusion

slides.title: TelePreview —— long-horizon Experiment
slides.selectionReason: TelePreview detailed framework, experiments, and conclusion

slides.title: TelePreview —— Conclusion We introduce TelePreview, a low-cost AR-augmented teleoperation system that delivers real-time, robot-centric previews to improve precision and safety across diverse platforms. Our user studies
slides.selectionReason: TelePreview detailed framework, experiments, and conclusion
